payload.text: can you create a pdf of my application.

**Analyzing Limitations**

I'm currently focused on the limitations of my role. It's clear that the user's core request is a PDF generation, and I recognize that's outside my skillset. I'm primarily an Angular engineer, designed to understand and modify code, not create tangible files.


**Clarifying the Boundaries**

I've decided to tell the user that making a PDF directly is beyond my capabilities. My forte lies in code, not document creation. While I grasp the request for a PDF, I need to be transparent about my limitations as an Angular engineer. However, I can explain the 'Print to PDF' option, a feature in any web browser, and offer step-by-step instructions. This approach respects my skillset while still addressing the user's needs.


**Declining the Request**

I'm now fully committed to the refusal. The user needs a PDF, which, regrettably, is beyond my current skill set. My focus as an Angular engineer is squarely on code manipulation, not document generation. I will explain the limitations transparently, then outline how the user can generate the PDF using their browser's built-in "Print to PDF" functionality. I'll provide detailed, easy-to-follow steps to make the process as smooth as possible.

I understand you'd like a PDF version of your application. That's a great idea for sharing or offline viewing.

However, as a code-generating AI, I don't have the capability to render the live application and convert it into a PDF file directly. My expertise is in writing and modifying the source code that builds the app.

The good news is that you can easily create a high-quality PDF of the application yourself using your web browser. Here are the steps for most modern browsers like Chrome, Edge, or Firefox:

1.  **Run the Application:** Open your Arogya application in your web browser so you can see it live.
2.  **Open the Print Dialog:** Press `Ctrl+P` (on Windows/Linux) or `Cmd+P` (on Mac) to open the print menu.
3.  **Change the Destination:** Look for the "Destination" or "Printer" setting. Click on it and select **"Save as PDF"** from the list.
4.  **Adjust Settings (Optional):** You can adjust settings like layout (portrait/landscape) and margins if you wish, but the defaults are usually fine.
5.  **Save the PDF:** Click the "Save" button. You'll be prompted to choose a name and location on your computer for the PDF file.

This will create a PDF document that looks just like your application, which you can then share or keep for your records.

payload.text:
act as a professional developer and can you add all the warmups for the yoga listed bellow and add missing ones. also can you add a 3 column content of what all warmup exercises are there in the entire list.

EYES
Eye Rotations (Netra Vyayam) — Circular movements of the eyes clockwise and counterclockwise, strengthening the extraocular muscles, relieving eye strain, and improving focus.
Horizontal Eye Movements — Side-to-side tracking that stretches the medial and lateral rectus muscles, enhancing peripheral awareness.
Vertical Eye Movements — Up-and-down tracking engaging the superior and inferior rectus muscles, releasing tension from prolonged screen exposure.
Diagonal Eye Movements — Corner-to-corner tracking activating the oblique muscles and improving comprehensive ocular coordination.
Palming (Trataka Rest) — Cupping warm palms over closed eyes to relax the optic nerve, soothe eye fatigue, and calm the nervous system.
Distant and Near Focus (Shifting Gaze) — Alternating focus between near and far objects to exercise the ciliary muscles and improve accommodation.

FACE & JAW
Lion's Breath (Simhasana Pranayama) — Wide mouth opening with tongue extension, releasing tension in the jaw, face, and throat while activating facial muscles.
Jaw Circles — Gentle circular movements of the mandible, releasing the masseter and temporalis muscles, relieving TMJ tension.
Facial Massage — Self-massage of the forehead, temples, cheeks, and jawline to release fascial tension and stimulate circulation.

HEAD & NECK
Neck Rolls — Gently releases tension in the neck and upper shoulders, improving cervical flexibility and relieving stiffness from static postures.
Neck Side Stretches — Lateral flexion of the cervical spine, lengthening the scalenes and upper trapezius while calming the nervous system.
Chin Tucks — Engages deep cervical flexors, correcting forward head posture and stabilizing the cervical spine.
Neck Flexion and Extension — Forward and backward movements warming the anterior and posterior cervical muscles.

SHOULDERS & UPPER BACK
Shoulder Rolls — Warms up the shoulder joints and releases tightness in the upper back, trapezius, and levator scapulae.
Arm Circles — Progressive circular movements that lubricate the glenohumeral joint and activate the rotator cuff muscles.
Eagle Arms (Garudasana Arms) — Deep stretch for the rhomboids, posterior deltoids, and space between the shoulder blades.
Cow Face Arms (Gomukhasana Arms) — Opens the shoulders through internal and external rotation, stretching the triceps and anterior deltoids.
Thread the Needle — Rotational stretch targeting the thoracic spine and posterior shoulder while releasing upper back tension.

ARMS, WRISTS & HANDS
Wrist Circles — Rotational movements lubricating the radiocarpal joint and warming the forearm flexors and extensors.
Wrist Flexion and Extension — Forward and backward bending of the wrists, preparing them for weight-bearing postures.
Finger Spreads and Fists — Alternating extension and flexion of the fingers, activating intrinsic hand muscles and improving grip strength.
Prayer Stretch (Reverse Prayer) — Deep wrist and forearm stretch opening the carpal tunnel and anterior forearm.

SPINE & TORSO
Cat-Cow Pose (Marjaryasana/Bitilasana) — Dynamic spinal flexion and extension that warms the entire vertebral column, improves intervertebral mobility, and stimulates cerebrospinal fluid circulation.
Seated Torso Twists — Warms up the thoracic spine, obliques, and intercostal muscles, preparing them for deeper rotational postures.
Standing Side Bends — Lateral spinal flexion that lengthens the quadratus lumborum, obliques, and intercostals while opening the ribcage.
Spinal Waves — Fluid undulating movements through the entire spine, awakening segmental vertebral articulation and core engagement.
Pelvic Tilts — Gentle anterior and posterior tilting that warms the lumbar spine, activates the deep core, and releases lower back tension.

CORE & ABDOMEN
Supine Knee-to-Chest (Apanasana) — Gentle compression that massages the abdominal organs, releases the lower back, and warms the hip flexors.
Reclined Twists (Supta Matsyendrasana) — Rotational stretch for the obliques, spinal erectors, and outer hips while gently massaging internal organs.
Bridge Lifts (Setu Bandhasana Prep) — Dynamic pelvic lifts that warm the glutes, hamstrings, and spinal extensors while activating the core.

HIPS & PELVIS
Hip Circles — Standing or tabletop circular movements that lubricate the acetabulofemoral joint and warm the surrounding musculature.
Butterfly Pose (Baddha Konasana) — Opens the inner thighs and groin, warming the hip adductors and external rotators.
Figure Four Stretch — Targets the piriformis and deep external hip rotators, releasing tension in the posterior hip capsule.
Low Lunge (Anjaneyasana) — Deep hip flexor stretch warming the psoas, iliacus, and rectus femoris while opening the anterior hip.
Lizard Pose (Utthan Pristhasana) — Intensified hip opener targeting the hip flexors, adductors, and groin region.
90-90 Hip Switches — Dynamic internal and external rotation transitions that warm all planes of hip movement.

LEGS & THIGHS
Downward-Facing Dog (Adho Mukha Svanasana) — Full-body stretch energizing the arms, shoulders, spine, hamstrings, and calves while building heat throughout.
Standing Forward Fold (Uttanasana) — Lengthens the entire posterior chain including hamstrings, calves, and spinal erectors.
Pyramid Pose Prep (Parsvottanasana Prep) — Targeted hamstring and calf stretch with hip-squaring alignment.
Leg Swings (Front-to-Back) — Dynamic movements warming the hip flexors, hamstrings, and improving hip joint range of motion.
Leg Swings (Side-to-Side) — Lateral dynamic stretches warming the adductors and abductors.
Chair Pose (Utkatasana) — Activates and warms the quadriceps, glutes, and core while building heat in the lower body.

KNEES & LOWER LEGS
Knee Circles — Gentle rotational movements lubricating the knee joint and warming the surrounding ligaments and menisci.
Calf Raises — Activates the gastrocnemius and soleus, warming the Achilles tendon and improving ankle stability.
Standing Quad Stretch — Lengthens the quadriceps and hip flexors while improving balance and proprioception.

ANKLES & FEET
Ankle Circles — Rotational movements lubricating the talocrural joint and warming the muscles of the lower leg.
Toe Stretch (Kneeling) — Extends and warms the plantar fascia, toe flexors, and intrinsic foot muscles.
Foot Flexion and Extension — Alternating dorsiflexion and plantarflexion to warm the tibialis anterior and calf muscles.
Toe Splays and Scrunches — Activates the intrinsic foot muscles, improving foot dexterity and grounding awareness.

act as a professional developer and can you add all the warmups for the yoga listed bellow and add missing ones. also can you add a 3 column content of what all warmup exercises are there in the entire list.
EYES
Eye Rotations (Netra Vyayam) — Circular movements of the eyes clockwise and counterclockwise, strengthening the extraocular muscles, relieving eye strain, and improving focus.
Horizontal Eye Movements — Side-to-side tracking that stretches the medial and lateral rectus muscles, enhancing peripheral awareness.
Vertical Eye Movements — Up-and-down tracking engaging the superior and inferior rectus muscles, releasing tension from prolonged screen exposure.
Diagonal Eye Movements — Corner-to-corner tracking activating the oblique muscles and improving comprehensive ocular coordination.
Palming (Trataka Rest) — Cupping warm palms over closed eyes to relax the optic nerve, soothe eye fatigue, and calm the nervous system.
Distant and Near Focus (Shifting Gaze) — Alternating focus between near and far objects to exercise the ciliary muscles and improve accommodation.
FACE & JAW
Lion's Breath (Simhasana Pranayama) — Wide mouth opening with tongue extension, releasing tension in the jaw, face, and throat while activating facial muscles.
Jaw Circles — Gentle circular movements of the mandible, releasing the masseter and temporalis muscles, relieving TMJ tension.
Facial Massage — Self-massage of the forehead, temples, cheeks, and jawline to release fascial tension and stimulate circulation.
HEAD & NECK
Neck Rolls — Gently releases tension in the neck and upper shoulders, improving cervical flexibility and relieving stiffness from static postures.
Neck Side Stretches — Lateral flexion of the cervical spine, lengthening the scalenes and upper trapezius while calming the nervous system.
Chin Tucks — Engages deep cervical flexors, correcting forward head posture and stabilizing the cervical spine.
Neck Flexion and Extension — Forward and backward movements warming the anterior and posterior cervical muscles.
SHOULDERS & UPPER BACK
Shoulder Rolls — Warms up the shoulder joints and releases tightness in the upper back, trapezius, and levator scapulae.
Arm Circles — Progressive circular movements that lubricate the glenohumeral joint and activate the rotator cuff muscles.
Eagle Arms (Garudasana Arms) — Deep stretch for the rhomboids, posterior deltoids, and space between the shoulder blades.
Cow Face Arms (Gomukhasana Arms) — Opens the shoulders through internal and external rotation, stretching the triceps and anterior deltoids.
Thread the Needle — Rotational stretch targeting the thoracic spine and posterior shoulder while releasing upper back tension.
ARMS, WRISTS & HANDS
Wrist Circles — Rotational movements lubricating the radiocarpal joint and warming the forearm flexors and extensors.
Wrist Flexion and Extension — Forward and backward bending of the wrists, preparing them for weight-bearing postures.
Finger Spreads and Fists — Alternating extension and flexion of the fingers, activating intrinsic hand muscles and improving grip strength.
Prayer Stretch (Reverse Prayer) — Deep wrist and forearm stretch opening the carpal tunnel and anterior forearm.
SPINE & TORSO
Cat-Cow Pose (Marjaryasana/Bitilasana) — Dynamic spinal flexion and extension that warms the entire vertebral column, improves intervertebral mobility, and stimulates cerebrospinal fluid circulation.
Seated Torso Twists — Warms up the thoracic spine, obliques, and intercostal muscles, preparing them for deeper rotational postures.
Standing Side Bends — Lateral spinal flexion that lengthens the quadratus lumborum, obliques, and intercostals while opening the ribcage.
Spinal Waves — Fluid undulating movements through the entire spine, awakening segmental vertebral articulation and core engagement.
Pelvic Tilts — Gentle anterior and posterior tilting that warms the lumbar spine, activates the deep core, and releases lower back tension.
CORE & ABDOMEN
Supine Knee-to-Chest (Apanasana) — Gentle compression that massages the abdominal organs, releases the lower back, and warms the hip flexors.
Reclined Twists (Supta Matsyendrasana) — Rotational stretch for the obliques, spinal erectors, and outer hips while gently massaging internal organs.
Bridge Lifts (Setu Bandhasana Prep) — Dynamic pelvic lifts that warm the glutes, hamstrings, and spinal extensors while activating the core.
HIPS & PELVIS
